So, and we'll get to a document later in your book. So I have a school of thought in my role as a marketing guy called Magnetic Marketing. And it's now 30-some-odd years old. And it has a life of its own. It has an identity of its own. It really is nothing more than taking the tenets of mail order. There's this thing called mail order, NUDSUR. This goes on your list with typewriters and Est. There's this thing called mail order. It's not M-A-L-E, you don't order a guy that exists. But it's how people used to buy things. It's sort of like Amazon, but with envelopes and stamps. A stamp is a... So it really is the tenets of mail order moved to non-mail order businesses. That's really all there is. But if you say it that way, not only is it not particularly interesting, you run the risk of it sounding horribly antiquated and nobody wanted anything to do with it. Even 25 years ago, you ran that kind of a risk. Plus everybody else could talk about it. So Godin really, permission marketing, followed magnetic marketing by at least ten years, and I suspect strongly there was some emulation. But so one of these key things here is you will have your own school of thought in the field in which you are in, about which you can then be a thought leader if you are going to get to legendary status. And in different fields, by the way, a lot of times the legends are legends only to the people in that field, more so than they are to the public. Billy Crystal was on the Leno show last night with Leno, and he was talking about when they were starting out in comedy, and Leno didn't live in New York, he lived in Boston, so any comedian who went to Boston slept on the floor in Leno's crappy apartment. The door was always open. And he said there was hardly any furniture in the place, but the thing he most remembers is above the bed there was a poster of this particular comedian, and he named him, I won't name him for you, you can go try and find him if you want to. There certainly are comedians who never, they never made the kind of money Leno's made, they've never had the kind of public fame, but in the world of comedians these guys are legends. David Steinberg would be in that category, by the way, and Steinberg, a lot of you wouldn't know Steinberg as a stand up guy, and you won't know that he became a producer behind produced Seinfeld, many of the Seinfeld episodes, for example. But among comedians, Steinberg is like a god, and that exists in a lot of these fields because they have a particular school of thought that they can enunciate about why and how things ought to be done. I am just going to, you want a break, don't you? It was on your little list. Okay. I have, one of my sayings is weak bladders are very expensive because I won't need one all day, but I know ordinary mortals do. So we'll take, I've got 20 after on my magic clock up here, so we'll take 15, but only 15. I'll start back, whether you're back or not, because I get paid whether you're here or not. So I'll start back, and I'm going to try and zap as fast as I can so we have as much time for discussion and Q&A and all that after lunch as I possibly can. So we'll just arbitrarily stop here and resume in 15 minutes. So at your leisure, but you have a, starting on page 16, you have notes here for your own consumption, chapter out of a new book. It is really about positioning yourself with information product. I want to just sort of leap to demonstration and dramatization. So demonstration is a, again, a not well understood, important part of creating legend and having legend told. So for many years in martial arts, any of you have your kids in a martial arts academy somewhere. So for many years, good thing to do with your kids. I'll endorse the industry. If you find the right one, they actually like learn discipline and civility and all of that. They'll say, sir, they'll put the dishes away after dinner without being asked. It's a remarkable thing. For many years, the demonstration of martial arts is what? Breaking boards. And so every martial arts demonstration, like ever, is somebody with their bare hand breaking boards, breaking concrete blocks, breaking something. And the bigger the stack, the more impressive the demonstration and so forth. So that industry built itself on the strength of a demonstration, basically co-opted and used by everybody. The thing about physical demonstration, particularly that which appears to have the risk that it could go wrong. So if you take, so nobody will go to the circus and pay money to see somebody walk across a two by four on the ground. It's walking across a wire 50 feet in the air that starts to get people excited. If you take the net away and you stretch the wire across a Grand Canyon and you have the guy walk across that with no net and if he falls, he's dead, then people are really excited. Understand it's the same demonstration. But it seems very, very different because it is more dramatic. So demonstration and dramatization go together in ways that build legends. I have a video clip I show often in seminars. I didn't bring it, but I'm going to describe it to you. Houdini, and if you are really interested in this topic, certainly one of the people you should make a point of studying is Houdini. Because he made himself a legend to the point that pretty much everybody still knows that name now, long after he's been dead. And almost every competent professional magician then and now will tell you they would rank his skills as a magician actually somewhere between moderate and disappointing. He has a person who understood this process. He hit every base beautifully. And so this clip I show, when you went to the movie theaters, if you were my parents' age, you saw newsreels before the movies. So now we see 500 previews and sometimes a charity thing and they come around with a bucket. But you used to see a cartoon and a couple of newsreels, particularly during World War II. And before that. And so Houdini created fake newsreels. Now this is near and dear to Mr. Nanton and Mr. Dick's heart. Because I suppose it's ungenerous, but generally when people ask me what these guys do, I say they create fake fame. Which sometimes becomes real fame, but it's really not the intent. So Houdini created newsreels, but he didn't rely on the news media to do it. He hired his own camera crew and he had them film him like a newsreel and then gave it to news agencies and gave it to theaters and gave it to whoever wanted to use it. And so there's these two newsreels of Houdini doing the straight jacket escape. Now the straight jacket escape is a very common and was then a very common magic trick. You can buy it in magic stores actually where it's rigged for you the way they would have rigged it themselves. And pretty much every magician of his time was already doing straight jacket escapes. So it's one of the tricks everybody did just like every kid magician does the rabbit in the hat. So it was that. So how do you do that trick? So the newsreel one time has him and he has gathered a crowd, a huge crowd, because you don't want to shoot a newsreel with nobody watching you, in the street and he is suspended by his ankles from a crane off the roof of a big department store in the straight jacket and he does the straight jacket escape hanging upside down by his ankles, swinging from the crane in the air. The other one, huge crowd, straight jacket, he dives off of a pier into the ocean in Atlantic City and does the straight jacket escape in the ocean. Incidentally, the second one is easier than the first one because they don't actually see you doing it. But even the one on the crane is actually easier to do than the one everybody does standing in front of you on the stage because you look at him five stories up. Unless you've got binoculars with you, you can't really see what, so he doesn't have to be as actually technically competent. So in the show Deadwood, no, not Deadwood, I'm sorry, wrong period of time, Boardwalk Empire. So in Boardwalk Empire, season two, so how many of you Boardwalk Empire? HBO, really? That's kind of much Boardwalk Empire. So Boardwalk Empire is like the Sopranos but in prohibition and it's relatively historically accurate. It's about Atlantic City. And so Houdini had a brother, his name was Hardin, who sometimes worked for him but often performed on his own. So there's a scene in this Boardwalk Empire episode where Hardin has performed a show and now they're all up in the Big Shot's suite like Nick would hold a little party and eight or ten or twenty dignitaries from the town and the Big Shot and Hardin are there. And somebody says to him, because Hardin did the straight jacket escape, and they say to him, so they refer to this actual historical thing, they say, we saw the newsreel of your brother doing that escape. It was incredible, just hanging upside down, swinging in the breeze from a crane in front of a department store. And Hardin, thoroughly pissed off with great umbrage, says it's the same effing trick. And it is, it's the same trick. But it's infinitely more dramatic. So demonstration and dramatization go together. So in the, somebody was in the health, wellness, fitness, who, you, okay. So pray tell me you're familiar with Jack LaLaine. Okay, it's okay if nobody else is. Okay, no harm, no foul. Because, so Carl and I took the big Alaska cruise a few years ago. And it was great, by the way. But the one thing that thoroughly pissed me off is you're supposed to see polar bears. This is a big part of this story. As you're going to go through the inside straight, and there's going to be polar bears on the ice. So I am up on the deck. It's 12 degrees below zero. I'm out there with my binoculars. You know, I want to see polar bears. And we took this entire cruise and it dawned on me, what stupid polar bear is going to be sitting out there in the middle of nowhere. You know, Gore had to get one picture and use it over and over and over. Yeah, one polar bear, dumb enough to sit on a melding ice. These polar bears are all in town. They're at the bar. And they will be excited to be there. And they will engage in civil conversation with you. Well, to a parent of a college-aged person, this is fire walk. This is, huh? They're pretty much dressed kind of nice. They're like cleaned, but they're not like Mormon, you know, where they all look the same. They're all, so they're, it's a dramatic demonstration. In our world, now, so let's make it micro, okay? So let's take it to micro levels of something we might be able to do. So in my world, J.A. Abraham, Halbert, I, we all do a version of the hot seat without a net. And that is at random taking somebody out of the audience, bringing them up here, putting them on a stool, letting them describe a business problem or task or objective or something they want to accomplish. So like what you said to me on the break, sir, where, okay, I'm going to real estate coach, how do I differentiate myself from all these others? So he would come up here and do that. And we would, with no notes, no preparation, like a magician, we've never met before, right? I didn't know what you were going to talk about before you came up here. No, you didn't know, right? I'm completely unprepared, and I would go to the light board and I would magically create his ad, or I would give him his six-part marketing strategy. Or I would, if he had a problem, I would give him the seven solutions to his problem, okay? That's the marketing consultant's fire walk. That's what that is. It's a demonstration, and it's dramatic because it could go horribly wrong. That's what, that's where the drama is. It could go horribly wrong. If you don't do demonstration that could go horribly wrong, you miss half the equation that causes people to tell everybody about what they saw you do. Beneficial association. So, everybody sort of understands, okay, I'm judged by the company I keep. This is why it's very important, you know, the Chinese proverb is, lie down with dogs or wake up with fleas. It's very important if you do this sort of thing, you exercise more control than they did about who they put in front of their group. It's important that you give a lot of thought to who you expose people to, who look up to you or are supposed to look up to you, what connections you make for people, because you will ultimately be held responsible. The second part of the equation is, you are judged by the company that keeps you. So, I told you earlier the breakthrough for the success events, the Peter Low events that I was on for nine years. So, it started with just Peter. Then it was Peter and Zig, then it was Peter and Zig and me, and half days, and we might have 200 people, 300 people, 400 people in the room, and then we got 18,000 people in the room. Ronald Reagan was the breakthrough. Nobody in the dignitary, important, and influential person category, countless numbers of them said no to Peter when they were approached to be on this event, because they all looked down on it when they understood what it was. It's a bunch of motivational speakers, which, two vast percentages of the population, are right in the same place as used car sales guys and lawyers. So, and it's this big circus thing, and there's going to be a lot of people in an auditorium, and during the day people are going to sell them shit, and the guy goes, no. Right? When Reagan did it, now the line out the door of everybody who wants to do it. Reagan changed everything. It's good enough for Reagan, it's good enough for me. In fact, I went on that thing, and it suppressed fees. Not only did it provide them, it allowed Peter to get them at affordable rates, because they wanted to be on it. It was a huge, so big breakthroughs for me were that. That thing was huge for me. It was a feeder system, unlike any feeder system, because it was not bought and paid for, visibly anyway, to your point, to what you do. You're on with all these famous people, including world leaders and business leaders and CEOs and so forth, the list is long, for beneficial association. You're in an incredible environment, and everybody is talking about it. And another breakthrough for me was the old original Success Magazine, Media. Very different than the Success Magazine you see today, although that can't be a breakthrough for certain people. Nite and Gale-Conan catalog. In its day, so how many of you bought something from Nite and Gale-Conan? Okay, so in its day, it was the place. For personal development, sales training, the experts of the experts were in Nite and Gale-Conan. So Nite and Gale-Conan was huge for me. But maybe the biggest thing was not the company I kept, but the company that kept me. So in 1987, I worked on the first infomercial of a company called Guthe-Renker, which most people now know. Then they were intellectual property. The first show was Think and Grow Rich, hosted by Fran Tarkett, and who used to play for the Minnesota Vikings, probably before you were born, but he's a little short guy, somewhat like Russell Wilson, CLC Hawks. Then the second show was Personal Power One, the first Tony Rahman show, also hosted by Fran Tarkett. So I worked on the first Think and Grow Rich show in 1987, and I've had a consulting relationship with these guys ever since. Now, today they're a... Last year I think they did 1.8, 1.9 billion, and they're primarily actually a cosmetical company that in part uses infomercials. They hardly ever do intellectual property anymore. They're best known for proactive, the acne-glob, Victoria Principal Skincare, et cetera. So in... As they became huge, and they became huge, quick, they're what I call the ultimate cachet client. That because they use me, everybody else in their world wants to use me. People came who really didn't even know why. It's just, you must be rubbing their head or something, so rub my head. And how much will you charge to rub my head? I mean, that's literally what happens. People sell their companies to Warren Buffett, why? Because he's Warren Buffett. And give it a choice for selling to Warren for one price, or selling to a private equity firm nobody ever heard of for a higher price a whole lot of people take. I'll take Warren in the lower price, because he's Warren Buffett. So beneficial association... In the National Speakers Association, I told you, I was a very divisive figure. NSA was a subset of the professional speaker market, and it was a market for me. To tell the story very quickly, I was making 100 grand a year. My first year in speaking, doing it all wrong. And I finally went to an NSA event to think, and I'm going to up my game, as some of you said this morning, and I'm going to see how not to do this stupid. And I was horribly depressed by the end of the first day, because it became very evident to me, first of all, everybody's a better speaker than me, but hardly anybody was making any money, and they certainly weren't making 100,000. And there was nothing for me to get from them that would help me make a half a million, because this was beyond anybody's... And pretty much hallway conversation was a half a million. Are you kidding? 40 years, right 400 bucks, kiss everybody's ass, get an agent, all the stuff I didn't want to do. So... Yeah. So I came back. I got my first call out of it on the airplane, on the way back, by the way. Got by the ambassador Crabb, who used to be Tarzan, if you go back and buck Rogers. So I came back now. And then I went to, okay, I don't know much, but I know more than these dufuses about how to make money. And so I started a newsletter and I started consulting, and I was very divisive. I then bought a failing company to produce audio cassettes for everybody, and I had that mess hang on. So Nito Cabane, who you now know and will see at High Point University, Nito was then the youngest hot-shot star speaker in the NSA world, and that's a nonprofit association, so they elect their officers. And he got elected president of NSA. I deduced that I could leapfrog to influence with a lot of people. Not everybody, because I'm going to be divisive. But I could leapfrog to influence with a lot of people who would pay me no attention, unless this arm was stitched to Cabane's arm somehow. I bought it. It's that simple. And often people don't understand how to spend money in ways that allow them to leapfrog. So I wanted everybody talking about me, and not everybody talking about me as the evil idiot who had shown up and was saying bad things about NSA. So Nito and I basically, business arrangement. We produced a ton of audio product for him at cost, no profit. We paid him fees. He did a tour with me. I'd eat at four or five cities, I forget which. He stuck his neck out, not just for money, but because he actually believed in what we were doing, but for money. And so I bought Beneficial Association. I cannot tell you how many times this same principle. So for five minutes, we're not going to record. Just turn it off however you need to turn it off. So the mystery novel that's in front of you, now that's not a money-making. And back to the Peter Lo thing, Zigg and I had the best splits. That thing is no fee, nobody got speaking fees except the celebrities who didn't sell anything. The rest of us were there selling stuff. And everybody had a 40-60 split, 40 to them, 60 to the house, Zigg and I had it the other way around. So I was getting 60, 40. Now, however, after a year, and I understood what it was, that I had, if Peter had come and said, we need 100% of the revenue, the only way we can keep this thing going is you got to give us 100% of the money, you got to pay the cost of all the product you sell, you pay your own airfare, you pay everything, you want to stay on a tour. I just said, where do I sign? Because the legend building, as well as to your funnel point, the actual acquisition of customers, was worth far more than the money made that day doing that thing. This book will not, I won't make back the 20. I bought a reading cow for the same reason. The story to me is worth, not the story I just told you, the other story, is worth more than the money. So beneficial association. A media strategy. All legends actually have 1215 okay with you? All legends actually have a media strategy. Batman was built with a media strategy. Batman is built today with a media strategy. Jesus Christ had a media strategy. We'll talk about media strategy in depth about last thing I'll do after lunch, but know that it must exist. Target audience and territory. Target audience and territory. So this gets to being purposeful about your legend building, your credibility, your authority, your celebrity, everything you do. So for me, for example, I've often said I would rather be in Success, the old Success magazine, four times in a year than be on Oprah. I don't care about being on Oprah. Literally, these days, if she was still on real TV, because we're rapidly getting to Oprah Who, but if she was still on real TV, I don't know that I'd want to spend the money to get in my private jet and fly to Chicago to do the show. But if investors' business daily was going to do a profile about me, fire up the plane. So, and I gave him this answer about differentiate. You differentiate by either showing up like nobody else in the same place, or showing up in a place nobody else is. So I have a wealth advisor right now that works only with people who sell companies, 10 million to 50 million. Full-page ad where you'd expect it in Forbes, full-page ad in Cowboys & Indians magazine, which is a magazine for people with a crap load of money who like to decorate their house in Cowboys & Indians style and decorate their trophy wife that they exchanged for the other wife when they sold the company in Cowboys & Indians stuff. So the magazine's full of real expensive jewelry, real expensive women's clothes, some men's clothes, real expensive real estate, and real expensive home furnishings. He's the only financial guy in there. But his guy reads the magazine. Doesn't matter what a magazine is. So I show this often. I use it because to many people, it's somewhat shocking because of who is doing it. So, and it's a book I'm going to suggest you go get, and it's not mine. So Pastor Rick Warren, who you may know from Saddleback Church, you may know from his huge best-selling book, Purpose Driven Life. You might know him from having Obama and McCain together talking about gay rights or something during the first campaign on national TV. You may know him recently on tragedy and his family with his son. So he has a different book, Purpose Driven Life. It's called The Purpose Driven Church, which sounds nice because predatory marketing manual for pastors doesn't sound so nice. But that would be a more accurate title for this book. And it is incredibly instructive about this point, target audience and territory. So I'm just going to quickly show you two pages from the book, but you should go get it. So this is a page called Knowing Who... I'm only going to show you one, actually. There's two pages. I'm going to show you one. Knowing Who You Can Best Reach. And this has a list. There's another page that has a profile on it of their customer. This has a list of who they want to be famous to, to reach out to. I'm going to read you the list. There's ten categories of people on it. Second-time visitors to the church. First-time visitor, who knows. They came back twice. Now, understand, you have some really good tracking data. Second-time visitors to church. Close friends and relatives of new converts. We focus all the referral efforts on the newest converts circle of influence. Why? When are you most excited about anything? When you're a new convert. People going through a divorce. Now we're starting to get a little predatory. By the way, readily available public records. Number four, those who feel a need for a recovery program, alcohol, drugs, sex addition, et cetera. First-time frightened parents. How many of you have more than one kid? You guys all know this drill, right? The first one, you hear, ee, four rooms away. She's on 911 and you're running in there by the third kid. It's like, go out and eat dirt. Six, the terminally ill in their families. We are going to target our marketing and our message for our church to the terminally ill in their families. Seven, couples with major marriage problems. Not minor, major. Guns, bullets, house on fire. Public record, domestic dispute, police calls. Eight, parents with problem children. Nine, recently unemployed or those with major financial problems. And ten, the obvious new residents in the community. Now, 90% of all pastors in the country are only dealing with number ten. Now, the other nine might not be right for you as a pastor, as it is for them as a pastor. You might need your own list, but the important point is there's a list. And there's more not on the list for which he could care less if they know who he is than those on the list. I think this is one of the most important points about this whole game is how you stake out your territory, how you define your audience, who you decide to make yourself for, about, famous to, and get to tell and retell legends about you. So one of the wealthiest broadcasters of this time and any time, ever, is who? Rush Limbaugh. Limbaugh's entire business. Now, you can go back through the whole list. divisiveness, you go back through the whole list. Limbaugh's entire business is about preaching to the converted. If he changes a mind, nice, not what he's doing. There is a published interview where a college kid working on a term paper interviews him about his business. And I've never seen him enunciate it any place out, but he enunciates it there, that my business is all about preaching to the converted. In order to have an audience that my advertisers will pay for. If we happen to succeed in overthrowing the liberal movement in America, great, that's not what I'm about. I'm into preaching to the converted business. Understand it is infinitely easier to succeed preaching to the converted than preaching to virgins, preaching to skeptics, preaching to anybody and everybody. It's important if you want to get to legendary status in your lifetime to be a preaching to the converted person. Which means you have to figure out who that audience is. 15 on the list, wherever our list went. You still have it in front of you, even if I don't. Investment. So I gave you one example off-mic. This is something to invest in. Very few legends occur organically or accidentally. All sorts of heroic acts occur organically and accidentally, but are not remarked upon. Briefly. Christ, Houdini, everybody on our list, they invested an enormous amount of time and or money in building the legend. Disney, here we are. So Walt, if you've studied all the biographies and autobiographies, if you've really studied Disney as I have, Walt was not Uncle Walt. Walt was more like Steve Jobs is becoming known as in business. A very tough, very ruthless, spontaneously enraged, difficult human. They had a massive PR machine, and they still do today, to sanitize Walt. How many of you have seen saving Mr. Banks? The rest of you should see it. It's a great movie. It's a great entrepreneur movie. It's about, in part, how he got the owner of Mary Poppins who wanted no part of having a movie made at Disney to finally allow him to get a movie done. And as I pointed out in our newsletter, I think last month, amongst other things that he did to get that done, is he lied through his teeth to her. And cleaned up the mess later. He absolutely promised her there would be no animation in the movie, while they were working on the animation, for the movie. This is kindly Uncle Walt. There's a scene in the movie where she comes into the office and he's smoking, and he quickly puts a cigarette behind a thing, and says, we don't let the public see that. We don't let anybody know. But Walt, big smoker, big drinker. They invested huge amounts of money. And more and more and more, making and preserving the legend. You haven't seen this company walk away from it. If you're spending time here at all, it's Walt Walt Walt Walt Walt Walt everywhere. It's what Walt would do with statues of Walt's, it's exhibits of Walt. Everybody talks about Walt. How long has Walt been dead? How many CEOs of companies decades after they're dead is everybody? No, they understand they have a real asset in that legend. The other kind of investment that I must mention briefly is the kind you spent to be in this room, whatever it is you're spending to be in their group. If you aspire to anything, so in this case it is cobbling together authority, credibility, celebrity in a unique and uniquely powerful way. Then you must be a serious student of that thing. Highly successful people in all their fields are extremely serious students of the methodology, the process behind what it is that they do. And number 16, that investment in study includes the genealogy of what they do. So it's vital for Peek to know who popularized stock market investing and financial planning and retirement planning in 1930, who got that message across then. It's vital for her to understand est. Otherwise you have these huge gaps in your understanding of what has developed a category of commerce to where it is now. It makes it very difficult to predict where it will go and you miss all the commonalities of what drives it again and again and again and again. Everybody we now see excelling at something, they have predecessors. And within those predecessors there is a pattern. With clients, when I work with somebody in an industry or field that I know nothing about, it's one of the first things I do. Is I go back now as far in genealogy as I go and then work my way back to the present so that I have access to that pattern. If people are not spending serious money as serious students of the thing that they aspire to do, narrowly, I want to be the next woman, the first kind of a cross between Oprah and Tony and all of that, or I want to have the biggest practice of ex that's narrow, broader than what it takes to do that, like the thing we're talking about here. I say by their behavior you can know them and by their investment that you can know. So if you aren't pouring money into it, candidly, you're not real serious about it. Hefner and Disney have enormous things in common. The commonalities, and they basically were coming up at the same time in LA. The commonalities between the two of them are dramatic. Castle, mansion, mouse, bunny, fantasy, fantasy, et cetera, et cetera. And the other thing is, Hefner is virtually the male counterpart of Helen Gurley Brown, who built Cosmopolitan magazine, and roughly in the same time frame. So a lot of people just hear Hefner and Playboy, and they just think naked pictures, and that's the end of what they think about. But Hefner was far smarter than that, and about far more than that. And so this page 47 has to do with how he makes out a philosophical position, a belief system position, and essentially made, he's the first guy, way ahead of his time, who basically made porn mainstream acceptable. While Dad had all the other skin magazines hidden in the bottom drawer of the dresser, under the socks and the underwear, Mom let Playboy find its way onto the coffee table or into the living room. And so today there's enormous crossover, and nobody puts a raincoat over their head and goes to the store to buy it anymore. There's not separate theaters, et cetera. But in Hefner's time, he was breaking ground day after day after day after day, a very divisive figure. If you go back down our list, you got Hefner. And this particular page 47 gives you a glimpse. The word implementation was used many times this morning. It's somewhat of an overused word now. But this is the implementation page. These are the four short lists to work on. In marketing we make people work on things like unique selling proposition, unique value proposition, message market media triangle. In this category of authority, credibility, celebrity, legend, these are the four things I make people work on. So one is the list of knowns. What is it? A short list of things that you need or want known about you by your target audience. I'm going to show you my list in a second. But that's a very important list. And that's the list, you know, that should be up on the wall everywhere. You see it all the time. It governs your every conversation, your every writing, your every outpouring of information of any kind to any place is how many of these knowns is it making known or reinforcing. So these are the people, these are the things people tell other people about me, either generally or when they are going to refer somebody to me. Second, what are your stories that can be told? So you need a few key stories that are tight enough that people can remember them, tell them about you, and not screw them up. Three, who do you need to be telling your stories for you? So when I talked about a feeder system before, I mean I have a very deliberate rings around me and I know who I want to be, feeder system strategy, which we'll talk about in media strategy, I know who I want talking about me. I know who I want to be telling my story. And I know who I don't care about one way or the other, by the way. Most of the world is irrelevant to me. There's a relatively small piece of it and a relatively small group of people that are important to me to be telling my story a lot because they are telling it to people who are somebody who could come and give me a lot of money. How can these stories be memorialized? It's kind of a micro side point, because many of you are in businesses where you, in your core business that you mentioned, where you would be thinking referrals. The three main reasons why people don't get more referrals, a lot more referrals than they do, is one is because they operate by entitlement. They have an attitude and their staff has an attitude that because we do great estate plans or we do great dentistry or we do, and we heal the sick and we raise the dead and we do our core deliverable great, we're entitled to a bunch of referrals and therefore people will refer to us because we're entitled to it. Entitlement's a losing strategy anyway, you cut it. The second reason why you don't get more of them is because you don't get them stories to tell instead of, so here's what most professionals say, hey would you tell your friends about me? And the unspoken question is, what am I supposed to tell them? Right? But where's the script? And the third reason is they don't give them good tools. They don't give media that makes it easy for them and not awkward for them in order to tell somebody about you either proactively or when the opportunity arises. So how can these stories be memorialized? How can we put them into media forms, book, website, documentary, all the stuff you guys do, newsletter, position papers, weekly memos, so how many of your GKIC members? Okay, almost all of you. Okay, Diamond, keep your hands up if you're Diamond. Okay, good. If you're not, there's a two month free trial goal and you can upgrade if you go to the site. But so Diamond members, amongst other things, they get a weekly fact from me every week. So 52 times a year, you're hearing from me directly once a week. Well, that's a benefit and there is benefit. It's provocative, motivational, sometimes instructive. However, that's not why I do it. I do it for this reason because it gives me a media that I can keep reinforcing the knowns and keep reinforcing the stories so that Diamond members will tell people about me. So here's a list. I'm on page 50. So here's an example, okay, of the knowns list. So here's what I want people to know about me. Importantly, before they get to me. So on your left side, there's what we want customers and members, GKIC people to know. The right is clients, prospective clients. Somebody's going to come to me for consulting, copywriting, project work, make overall their marketing, that sort of thing, what we want them to know. Now some of those items are the same, a couple of marked, and they're in a couple of cases purposed differently. So if you look at the customer list first, what we want to know. One, we want them to know that I'm a legendary figure. Certainly amongst my peers and in certain fields. We want them to know about celebrity by association. So almost all Dan's story includes the nine years on the successive events and Reagan and the Hollywood celebrities and the, okay, because I want that association. I want them to know the autonomy story. A, it creates curiosity, envy. It's a demonstration of a big idea that I not only have for myself and price for myself, but I try and make it happen for my clients. I want them to know the no BS thing. This guy's very unvarnished, he's very coarse. He tells it like he sees it, etc. I want them to know pro wealth, pro entrepreneur. I want them to know I'm expensive. From the customer member standpoint, that's a whole value proposition. If a bunch of people are paying this guy 100 grand, 200 grand, 300 grand, a million dollars to create marketing programs for them and they're paying 19,000 dollars a day just to sit in a room and talk to them, you're getting a hell of a bargain here at 247 bucks a month and you're getting all this stuff. So that's a value argument. I want them to know there's long term retention. So we joke but I'm really not joking all the time about Dan's 100,000 dollar club, which is people who spend 100,000 dollars or more. And I'll do show hands sometimes at events. We'll recognize people in the newsletter who are in the 100,000 dollar club and customers want that recognition, they want that bragging right, they brag about it to each other. Now most marketers by the way would be horribly afraid of this. They would not want everybody running around talking about how they got 100,000 dollars from their customers because they think that's scary. I think it's enormously reassuring. We also use the word lifers. I talk about my lifers. Those are people who are in the spotlight. This is an old ad reference. You can get in the Roche Motel but you can't get out. So they got in and they've never left. They're there for 10 years, 20 years, 30 years, 35 years. On the client side, so legendary figures, same thing. However, on the client side, I want them to know about Cache Clients. So I want them to know about Guthrie Ranker, I want them to know about Health Source, I want them to know about my Cache Clients. I want them to know the autonomy story and I want them to know I'm difficult. So I want people telling everybody else, he's a pain in the ass to deal with but he's worth it. That's what I want said. Because I don't want it to be a big surprise when they finally come that I'm kind of a pain in the ass. It just cuts out a lot of conversation. It just seems like so many people taking instant dislike to me. Why do you think that is? And the guy says it just saves a lot of time. So I want to save a lot of time. I want that set. So that's my list of knowns that are the things I want communicated most.
